You can detect the CLI context inside your create_app function easily.
The flask command line tool invokes your app factory in a specific way.
You may check the command name to know if it is a CLI call.
This approach avoids needing extra environment variables for every run.
The flask command line passes the command name to the application context.
You can inspect the current command name within your factory function.
If the command name is not run then you are in a CLI context.
This logic allows you to set different configurations for CLI usage.
You might load a special config file when the command is a custom one.
The create_app function can branch based on the detected command name.
This branching lets you use different app instances for different modes.
You do not need to manually set variables before running the command.
The flask framework provides the necessary context to make this decision.
Your app can behave differently without any user input changes.
This solution keeps your command line usage simple and clean.
You can still use the standard flask run command for the server.
The CLI commands will automatically trigger the alternative configuration.
This method is robust and does not rely on external environment settings.
You can pass parameters to create_app by using the click context.
The click context is available when the command is being executed.
You can read the context to determine the exact command being run.
This gives you full control over how the app is initialized.
Your factory function becomes smart enough to handle both cases.
No extra steps are needed from the user to invoke the CLI.
